title: Apple Neural Engine, CoreML & Profiling on Apple Silicon
description: The Apple Neural Engine (ANE) is invisible to PyTorch and MLX — to reach it you go through CoreML. Learn the conversion path, heterogeneous CPU/GPU/ANE execution, and how to profile with Instruments and torch.mps.profiler.

sections.title: What is the Apple Neural Engine?
sections.content:
Coming soon — the ANE is a separate, specialized accelerator in every M-series chip that PyTorch and MLX cannot directly target.

<!-- TODO:
  - Hardware overview: 16-core ANE on M-series, fixed-function int8/fp16 matrix engines.
  - Why PyTorch can't dispatch to it: ANE is reachable only via the CoreML runtime (no public low-level API).
  - Power / thermal envelope: ANE is the most efficient option for sustained inference; GPU has higher peak FLOPS but draws more power and hits thermal throttling sooner on laptops.
  - When ANE actually wins (small-to-medium inference, latency-sensitive on-device), and when it doesn't (training, large models, custom ops).

sections.title: PyTorch -> CoreML -> ANE
sections.content:
Coming soon — the practical conversion pipeline from a trained PyTorch model to an ANE-eligible CoreML model.

<!-- TODO:
  - The path: PyTorch -> torch.jit.trace / torch.export -> coremltools.convert -> .mlpackage.
  - compute_units: ALL vs CPU_AND_NE vs CPU_AND_GPU; how to encourage the runtime to schedule onto the ANE.
  - Op compatibility: which PyTorch ops map cleanly, which fall back to GPU/CPU, how to inspect with the CoreML model viewer.
  - Heterogeneous execution: a single inference graph using all three (CPU + GPU + ANE).
  - Profiling: Instruments (Metal System Trace, CoreML template), xctrace, torch.mps.profiler for the parts still on GPU.
